name: Mirandese

ancestors.notes: Old Portuguese (Aviz-period) adstrate: as Portuguese consolidated as the state language, the Leonese-speaking communities of Miranda, Rionor, and Guadramil faced intensifying pressure. Portuguese orthographic conventions (lh, nh, ã) penetrated the writing tradition.

notes: Mirandese (mirandés), spoken in the Miranda do Douro municipality, northeastern Portugal. Classified as Asturleonese. Recognised as a regional/minority language in Portugal since 1999. Retains archaic Asturleonese features: diphthongisation of Latin short /e/ and /o/ (written ⟨iê/ie⟩, ⟨uô/uo⟩), palatal lateral /ʎ/ preserved (written ⟨lh⟩ in all positions; word-initial ⟨l⟩ before vowel = /ʎ/ in traditional lexicon per Convenção § L). Heavy Portuguese adstrate via official education and administration. Mirandese preserves the medieval four-way+ sibilant contrast that Portuguese lost, the hallmark of the language: (1) voiceless apico-alveolar /s̺/ ⟨s, ss⟩ and its voiced intervocalic counterpart /z̺/ ⟨-s-⟩; (2) voiceless (pre)dorso-dental /s/ ⟨c (before e/i), ç⟩ and voiced /z/ ⟨z⟩; (3) postalveolar /ʃ/ ⟨x⟩ and /ʒ/ ⟨j, g (before e/i)⟩; (4) the affricate /tʃ/ ⟨ch⟩ (still an affricate, unlike modern European Portuguese). NOTATION CONVENTION (matches the TigreGotico/mirandese_g2p expert gold): the apical/dorsal CONTRAST is written by marking only the apical series with the ̺ diacritic (⟨s⟩ = /s̺/, /z̺/) and leaving the dorso-dental series unmarked (⟨c, ç, z⟩ = plain /s/, /z/); this is IPA-standard, keeps the phonemic contrast explicit, and avoids the over-narrow laminal ̻ diacritic the gold does not use. ⟨ç⟩ is a fricative, not an affricate, and occurs word-finally (rapaç, lhuç) where Portuguese writes -z. ⟨j⟩ is /ʒ/ (western Iberian, never the Asturian palatal ʝ) — ⟨y⟩ is the glide /j/. INITIAL-L PALATALISATION (Leonese): word-initial ⟨l⟩ before a vowel = /ʎ/ (luç → [ˈʎus], Convenção § L; bare ⟨l⟩ /l/ survives only in loanwords, proper nouns and the article — a documented residual limit); ⟨lh⟩ = /ʎ/ and ⟨nh⟩ = /ɲ/. The trigraphs ⟨anh, enh, inh, onh, unh⟩ are given explicitly (oral vowel + /ɲ/, e.g. danho → [ˈdaɲu]) so the greedy nasal-vowel digraph does not swallow the /ɲ/ of a following ⟨nh⟩. Nasal digraphs ⟨an, en, in, on, un⟩ realise Portuguese-adstrate nasal vowels /ɐ̃ ẽ ĩ õ ũ/ before a consonant or at word boundary (brando → [ˈbɾɐ̃du]); before a vowel they are oral vowel + /n/. ALLOPHONY (post-lexical, allophone_rules): coda /n/ assimilates in place to a following labial ([m]) or velar ([ŋ]). Ibero-Romance intervocalic voiced-stop spirantisation (⟨b d g⟩ → [β ð ɣ]) is a REAL native process — voiced stops lenite to [β ð ɣ] everywhere except after a pause or a nasal (Mateus & d'Andrade 2000:11; Ferreira & Raposo 1999; mwl Wikipedia phonology, "voiced stops /b d ɡ/ MAY be lenited as fricatives [β ð ɣ]"). It is applied PER PHONEME on the basis of the expert-human TigreGotico/mirandese_g2p gold measured at true oral-intervocalic positions (post-nasal excluded): /b/ is spirant-dominant (β:9/b:5), so MWL_SPIRANT_B rewrites intervocalic /b/→[β] (a net win on the human gold, PER 0.1901→0.1851). /ɡ/ is already spirantised to [ɣ] intervocalically at the positional_graphemes layer (⟨g⟩ intervocalic → [ɣ]), so no allophone rule is needed — adding one only misfires after a diphthong glide (eigual → gold stop [ɐjˈɡwal]). /d/, however, remains STOP-dominant intervocalically in the gold (d:13/ð:7) — the documented Asturleonese conservatism/occlusion of /d/, and the reason the Wikipedia wording is hedged ("may be") — so /d/ keeps its positional default [d] and /d/→[ð] is DELIBERATELY NOT applied as a rewrite (a blanket /d/ rule regresses this expert gold); [ð] stays declared in the `allophones` inventory for the lattice / downstream mwl_phonemizer where lexical conditioning is available. Word-onset and post-nasal /b d ɡ/ keep the stop.

sources.author: Mateus, M.H.M. & d'Andrade, E.
sources.title: The Phonology of Portuguese
sources.notes: p. 11: the voiced stops /b d ɡ/ are lenited to the fricatives/approximants [β ð ɣ] in all positions EXCEPT after a pause or a nasal, where they surface as stops [b d ɡ]. Grounds the intervocalic spirantisation rule (MWL_SPIRANT_B) and the post-nasal/onset stop exception; the same process is described for Mirandese by Ferreira & Raposo 1999 and the mwl Wikipedia phonology ("voiced stops /b d ɡ/ may be lenited as fricatives [β ð ɣ]").

sources.author: Vasconcelos, J. Leite de
sources.title: Estudos de Philologia Mirandesa
sources.notes: Foundational description of Mirandese phonology, including the six-sibilant system.

sources.author: Ferreira, M.B. & Raposo, D. (coords.)
sources.title: Convenção Ortográfica da Língua Mirandesa
sources.publisher: Câmara Municipal de Miranda do Douro / Centro de Linguística da Universidade de Lisboa
sources.notes: Official orthography. § L: word-initial l before vowel = /ʎ/ in traditional lexicon (bare l occurs only in loanwords/proper nouns/article). § Nasalidade: an/en/in/on/un are nasal-vowel digraphs at word boundaries; they do NOT nasalise sequences before onset consonants. § Sibilantes: six-way contrast s̺/z̺/s̻/z̻/ʃ/ʒ. § Ditongos: iê/ie = /je/ (stressed /jɛ/); uô/uo = /wo/ (stressed /wɔ/). § Acento: paroxytone default; oxytone for -r/-l/-z/-ç/-in/-un/-on/-is/-us/-ns/-ão endings.

sources.author: Ferreira, M.B. & Raposo, D. (coords.)
sources.title: Primeiro Aditamento à Convenção Ortográfica da Língua Mirandesa
sources.publisher: Câmara Municipal de Miranda do Douro / Centro de Linguística da Universidade de Lisboa
sources.notes: February 2000 addendum. Key provision: Sendinese speakers may optionally write word-initial l- (not lh-) to represent /l/ rather than /ʎ/ (e.g., luna/lhuna). Sendinese monophthongisation of iê→i and uô→u also codified. Source: secondary (studosmirandeses.blogs.sapo.pt, cursodemirandes.wordpress.com); standalone PDF not located.

stress.notes: Convenção Ortográfica da Língua Mirandesa (1999) accentuation, Portuguese-based: paroxytone default; written accent (acute or tilde) overrides. Oxytone-attracting endings reflect Mirandese orthography: consonant-final r/l/z/ç (rapaç, Pertual, falar) and the final nasals written with -n in the Asturleonese manner — -in (camin, Sendin), -un, and the systematically oxytone -on class from Latin -ōnem (naçon, coraçon, Associaçon). Unmarked -an/-en verb endings stay paroxytone as in Portuguese -am/-em.

allophone_rules.notes: Nasal place assimilation (Mateus & d'Andrade 2000 §3; general Ibero-Romance): a coda /n/ agrees in place with a following labial stop/nasal, surfacing as [m].
Nasal place assimilation (Mateus & d'Andrade 2000 §3): a coda /n/ agrees in place with a following velar stop, surfacing as [ŋ].

Intervocalic spirantisation, /b/ → [β] between vowels (rabudo → [rɐˈβudu], haber → [ɐˈβeɾ], nuobo → [ˈnwoβu]). This is the general Ibero-Romance lenition of voiced stops: in northern/central EP and Mirandese, /b d ɡ/ are realised as the fricatives/approximants [β ð ɣ] everywhere EXCEPT after a pause or a nasal (Mateus & d'Andrade 2000:11), which is why this rule is conditioned on a preceding vowel (the MWL_NASAL_* environments and word onset keep the stop). The Mirandese-specific process is attested by Ferreira & Raposo 1999 and the mwl Wikipedia phonology ("voiced stops /b d ɡ/ may be lenited as fricatives [β ð ɣ]"). Applied per-phoneme on the evidence of the expert-human TigreGotico/mirandese_g2p gold, measured at true oral-intervocalic positions (post-nasal excluded): /b/ is spirant-dominant (β:9 vs b:5), so this rule is a net win on the human gold (11 words corrected vs 6 lexically-conservative loan stops such as 'brabo', 'alternatibo' left over-spirantised — the residual lexical variability the downstream mwl_phonemizer can condition on). /ɡ/ is NOT given an allophone rule: intervocalic ⟨g⟩ is already spirantised to [ɣ] at the positional_graphemes layer, and an additional /ɡ/→[ɣ] rewrite misfires after the glide of a falling diphthong (eigual → gold [ɐjˈɡwal], stop retained). /d/ is NOT spirantised: intervocalic /d/ is stop-dominant in the gold (d:13 vs ð:7), the documented Asturleonese conservatism of /d/, so its positional default correctly stays [d] and [ð] remains only a lattice candidate.
